4.4.a.info:
SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is the de facto standard protocol for monitoring and managing IP network devices. It was designed in the late 1980s as a simple way for a central Network Management Station (NMS) to read operational data from routers, switches, servers, and firewalls — and to receive unsolicited alerts when something breaks.

SNMP is UDP-based, never TCP. Two ports matter: UDP 161 for manager-to-agent polls (Get/Set) and UDP 162 for agent-to-manager notifications (Trap/Inform). UDP was chosen for low overhead — network management traffic shouldn't add congestion to the network it's monitoring.

Why SNMP still matters: every major monitoring platform — SolarWinds, PRTG, LibreNMS, Zabbix, Cacti, Nagios, Observium — speaks SNMP. Even newer streaming telemetry (gNMI, NETCONF, model-driven telemetry) has not displaced SNMP in most enterprises. On the CCNA, you are expected to know the three components, the two port numbers, the message types, and the security differences between v1, v2c, and v3.

Three versions in the wild: v1 (plaintext, deprecated), v2c (plaintext community strings, added GetBulk and Inform, most widely deployed), and v3 (user-based security with optional auth and encryption, required for anything touching the public internet or compliance audits).

hack.memory: SNMP = Simple Network Management Protocol. UDP only. Two ports: 161 (manager polls agent) and 162 (agent alerts manager). Three components: Manager, Agent, MIB. Three versions: v1 (old plaintext), v2c (plaintext + GetBulk + Inform), v3 (USM, the only one with encryption).
hack.practice: Write on a flashcard: 'SNMP ports? 161/162 UDP. Which direction? 161 manager→agent, 162 agent→manager. Which version encrypts? Only v3 at authPriv.' Review until instant recall — these appear as 1-2 second questions on the exam.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9 (network management). The #1 exam recall: SNMP is UDP, 161 queries, 162 notifications, v3 is the only secure version.

4.4.a.info:
The SNMP Manager, also called the Network Management Station (NMS), is the central brain of the monitoring system. It is the software application — SolarWinds Orion, PRTG, LibreNMS, Nagios, Zabbix, Observium — that runs on a dedicated server and talks to every managed device in the network.

What the manager does: (1) Polls — at a regular interval (typically every 1-5 minutes) the NMS sends Get or GetBulk requests to UDP port 161 on each device, reading OID values to build graphs and dashboards. (2) Receives traps — listens on UDP port 162 for unsolicited notifications when devices detect problems. (3) Writes configuration (optional) — with a read-write community or authPriv user, the manager can issue Set requests to modify values (shut an interface, change a hostname, enable/disable features).

Classic deployment: one NMS with a database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, or a time-series DB like InfluxDB) storing historical data, a web UI for dashboards, and an alert engine (email, SMS, Slack, PagerDuty). The NMS is usually centrally located in the data center with firewall rules allowing outbound UDP 161 to every managed device and inbound UDP 162 from them.

Scale considerations: polling thousands of devices every minute adds up. GetBulk (v2c+) lets the manager pull hundreds of OIDs in one packet instead of chaining GetNexts. Most modern NMS deployments disable v1 entirely, accept v2c only from trusted management VLANs, and require v3 authPriv for anything crossing untrusted paths.

hack.memory: Manager = the boss at HQ with the monitor wall. Polls every device on UDP 161, listens for traps on UDP 162, writes config via Set (if allowed). Common examples: SolarWinds, PRTG, LibreNMS, Nagios, Zabbix. One NMS watches hundreds-to-thousands of devices.
hack.practice: Install LibreNMS or PRTG Free in a VM. Add a Packet Tracer router or a real switch with 'snmp-server community public ro'. Watch the NMS poll every 5 minutes and graph CPU/interface counters. This single exercise makes SNMP click better than any reading.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which device initiates Get requests?' = Manager. 'Which device receives traps?' = Manager (on UDP 162).

4.4.a.info:
The SNMP Agent is software that lives on each managed device — router, switch, firewall, server, printer, UPS — and listens on UDP port 161 for queries from the manager. On Cisco IOS, the agent is built into the operating system; you enable and configure it with snmp-server global-config commands.

Agent responsibilities: (1) Respond to Get, GetNext, and GetBulk requests by reading values from the MIB and returning them. (2) Accept Set requests (only if the community or user has write access) and modify the local configuration accordingly. (3) Generate Trap or Inform messages when configured events occur (link down, authentication failure, temperature threshold) and send them to UDP 162 on the manager.

Configuration on Cisco IOS: minimal v2c agent is one line — snmp-server community MyString ro. That enables the agent, defines a read-only community string, and makes the device pollable. To send traps you add snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 MyString and snmp-server enable traps. Verify with show snmp (counters), show snmp community (communities defined), show snmp host (trap destinations).

Agent is always the responder, never the poller. The manager drives the conversation on port 161; the agent only speaks when spoken to — except for traps and informs, which the agent generates on its own when something breaks. That asymmetry is why two ports exist.

hack.memory: Agent = the on-duty employee at each device. Listens on UDP 161. Responds to Get (read) and Set (write). Fires traps on UDP 162 when things break. Built into Cisco IOS — enable with 'snmp-server community' or 'snmp-server user'. Never initiates polls.
hack.practice: On a Cisco router: 'snmp-server community PUBLIC ro' → 'snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 PUBLIC' → 'snmp-server enable traps'. Then 'show snmp' and 'show snmp host'. Shut an interface and watch a trap fire to the NMS.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'the agent runs on which device?' = the managed device (router/switch). 'The agent listens on which port?' = UDP 161.

4.4.a.info:
The MIB (Management Information Base) is a hierarchical, tree-structured database that defines every piece of data an agent can expose. Think of it as the schema the manager uses to ask intelligent questions: 'give me the value of this exact node in the tree.' Each node is identified by an OID (Object Identifier) — a dotted-number path like 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0.

Structure: the root is unnamed; the first branches are iso(1).org(3).dod(6).internet(1). Under internet you find mgmt(2).mib-2(1) — the standard MIB-II tree that every device implements. Under mib-2 sit familiar subtrees: system(1), interfaces(2), ip(4), tcp(6), udp(7). Vendors also publish enterprise MIBs under 1.3.6.1.4.1.<enterprise-number> — Cisco is 9, so Cisco-specific OIDs all start with 1.3.6.1.4.1.9.

Commonly polled OIDs:

  • 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1 — sysDescr (IOS version string)
  • 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3 — sysUpTime (device uptime in 1/100s)
  • 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.2 — ifDescr (interface names)
  • 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10 — ifInOctets (interface input bytes)
  • 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.16 — ifOutOctets (interface output bytes)
  • 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.8 — ifOperStatus (up/down)

MIB files are text files (ASN.1 notation) shipped by vendors that translate numeric OIDs into human-readable names. Monitoring tools load these MIB files so you can say 'ifInOctets' instead of remembering 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10. On Cisco you can browse MIBs at mibs.cloudapps.cisco.com.

hack.memory: MIB = the menu. Each menu item has a numeric code (OID) and a human name (ifInOctets). Standard menu = MIB-II under 1.3.6.1.2.1. Cisco's private menu = 1.3.6.1.4.1.9. Agent serves whatever OID the manager asks for. Think of sysUpTime OID '1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3' as 'internet.mgmt.mib-2.system.sysUpTime'.
hack.practice: Install the 'snmpwalk' tool (Linux/Mac). On a lab router with 'snmp-server community PUBLIC ro', run 'snmpwalk -v2c -c PUBLIC <router-ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1'. You'll see the entire system subtree translated into names. Try 'snmpget -v2c -c PUBLIC <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0' for just the uptime.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam tends to ask conceptually: 'what does the MIB contain?' = a tree of manageable objects (OIDs). You won't have to memorize specific OIDs, but knowing sysUpTime and ifInOctets exists is useful context.

4.4.a.info:
SNMP uses exactly two UDP ports and they are asymmetric — each direction has its own port. Memorize them as a pair.

UDP 161 — manager-to-agent (polls): every Get, GetNext, GetBulk, and Set goes from the NMS to the agent on destination port 161. The source port is ephemeral (usually > 32768). The agent replies from UDP 161 back to the NMS's ephemeral port. Firewalls between the NMS and managed devices must allow outbound UDP 161 from the NMS subnet and the return traffic (typically via stateful inspection).

UDP 162 — agent-to-manager (notifications): every Trap and Inform goes from the agent to the NMS on destination port 162. For Traps there is no reply — fire and forget. For Informs the NMS sends back an acknowledgment (still on UDP 162). Firewalls must allow inbound UDP 162 to the NMS from every managed device subnet.

Why UDP, not TCP? Management traffic should be lightweight and non-blocking. A three-way handshake per poll across thousands of devices would drown the NMS in state. UDP gives fast single-packet exchanges; if a packet is lost, the manager retries on the next poll cycle. The only exception is SNMP over TLS/DTLS (RFC 5953) which runs on TCP/UDP 10161 (queries) and 10162 (notifications) — rarely seen in CCNA scope, but know it exists.

Exam-anchored memorization: '161 in, 162 out.' The manager goes IN to the agent on 161. The agent sends notifications OUT to the manager on 162. 161 comes before 162 alphabetically and numerically, and polls (161) typically happen before traps (162) in a troubleshooting timeline.

hack.memory: 161 = manager pokes agent (polls). 162 = agent yells at manager (traps/informs). Both UDP. Mnemonic: '1-6-1 I poke One device, 1-6-2 TwO-way trap.' Or simply: 161 comes first (normal polling), 162 comes second (alarms).
hack.practice: On any Cisco device, run 'show snmp'. You'll see 'SNMP packets input/output' counters that increment as the NMS polls UDP 161. Shut an interface; 'show snmp' will show the trap counter increment as UDP 162 packets leave. This makes the ports concrete.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Guaranteed exam question: 'what UDP port does the manager use to poll the agent?' (161). 'What UDP port does the agent use to send traps?' (162). Drill until instant.

4.4.b.info:
The Get family of SNMP messages are the manager-to-agent read operations. All three are sent to UDP 161; the agent replies with the requested OID values.

Get — 'give me the value of this exact OID.' The manager specifies a single OID (e.g., 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0 for sysUpTime); the agent returns the value. Simple, precise, one question one answer. Used when you know exactly what you want.

GetNext — 'give me the value of the OID immediately after this one in the tree.' Lets the manager walk the MIB without knowing every OID in advance. You ask for the OID after 1.3.6.1.2.1.1 and get 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0; ask for the one after that and keep walking until the agent returns a value outside the requested subtree. This is how snmpwalk works under the hood.

GetBulk — 'give me the next N OIDs in one packet.' Introduced in SNMPv2c to solve the inefficiency of chaining dozens of GetNexts. The manager specifies a non-repeater count and a max-repetition count; the agent packs as many values as fit into one response. On a device with thousands of interfaces, GetBulk is 10-100x faster than GetNext. Not available in v1 — one of the big reasons to use v2c at minimum.

Response packet: all three trigger a Response PDU from the agent containing one or more varbinds (OID + value pairs). If the OID does not exist the response contains a noSuchObject error for that varbind.

hack.memory: Get = 'this one.' GetNext = 'the one after that' (walk). GetBulk = 'dump a section' (v2c+). All three are manager → agent on UDP 161. GetBulk is the speed upgrade — if a question says v1, GetBulk is NOT available; if it says v2c or v3, GetBulk IS available.
hack.practice: On Linux: 'snmpget -v2c -c PUBLIC <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0' (Get). 'snmpgetnext -v2c -c PUBLIC <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1' (GetNext). 'snmpbulkwalk -v2c -c PUBLIC <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.2' (GetBulk walking the interfaces subtree). Compare speeds — snmpbulkwalk is noticeably faster on large tables.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which SNMP message retrieves multiple values in one packet?' = GetBulk. 'Which is NOT available in v1?' = GetBulk (and Inform).

4.4.b.info:
Set is the manager-to-agent write operation. Instead of reading a value, the manager sends a Set PDU with an OID and a new value; the agent writes that value to the device and replies with a Response PDU confirming success or returning an error.

Write access is a privilege, not a default. For v2c the agent accepts Sets only if the community string is configured as rw (snmp-server community SECRET rw). For v3 the user must belong to a group with write access, and in practice that means authPriv level to prevent unauthorized modifications over the wire. Get-only communities ignore Set requests and return an error.

What can you Set? Any writable OID. Common examples: sysContact.0 (change the admin email), sysLocation.0 (update the building/rack), ifAdminStatus (shut or unshut an interface), and vendor-specific OIDs for things like saving config or reloading the device. Read-only OIDs like counters (ifInOctets) can never be Set — the MIB definition marks them read-only.

Security caution: a leaked RW community string on a v2c device is equivalent to full admin access, because an attacker can Set the router's IP, hostname, interface states, or even trigger a config save. This is the primary reason enterprises should never deploy v2c RW over untrusted paths. Use v3 authPriv or disable RW entirely and manage config via SSH/NETCONF instead.

hack.memory: Set = WRITE. Requires RW community (v2c) or an authPriv user with write permission (v3). Never expose RW on v2c to untrusted networks — it's effectively admin access without a password. Common real use: changing sysContact or shutting an interface. Uncommon in production — most shops use SSH/NETCONF instead.
hack.practice: Lab only: on a Cisco router, 'snmp-server community WRITE rw'. From a Linux host, 'snmpset -v2c -c WRITE <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.6.0 s "Rack 42"'. Then 'show snmp' or 'snmpget' sysLocation — it changed. Now remove the RW community immediately; do NOT leave this enabled.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which SNMP message modifies a value on the agent?' = Set. 'Requires what access level?' = read-write (RW community or v3 write privilege).

4.4.b.info:
A Trap is an unsolicited notification sent by the agent to the manager when a significant event occurs. Traps flow from the agent on UDP 162 to the manager. They are one-way and unacknowledged — the agent fires a single packet and moves on, with no mechanism to retry if the packet is dropped.

Typical trap events: link up / link down (linkUp / linkDown), authentication failure (authenticationFailure), cold start / warm start (coldStart / warmStart — device boot), and Cisco-specific traps like CPU threshold, temperature threshold, config change, and BGP neighbor state change. You enable them with snmp-server enable traps (all) or specific categories like snmp-server enable traps bgp.

Unreliable by design: because traps use UDP with no ACK, a lost packet is a lost alert. If the WAN link between the device and the NMS is congested at exactly the moment a critical trap fires, you may never know the event happened. This is why critical alerts should also be monitored via polling (threshold crossing detected by the NMS itself) or sent as Informs instead of Traps.

Configuration: snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 version 2c PUBLIC defines where to send traps. snmp-server enable traps turns on all trap categories. Verify with show snmp host (destinations) and show snmp (packet counters).

Versions: Traps exist in all three SNMP versions. In v1 they have a different PDU format (called Trap-v1) with fields like enterprise, agent-address, generic-trap, specific-trap, timestamp. In v2c and v3 they use a simpler SNMPv2-Trap PDU format.

hack.memory: Trap = FIRE AND FORGET. Agent → Manager on UDP 162. Unreliable — no ACK, no retry. If the trap packet is lost, the manager never knows the event happened. Enable with 'snmp-server enable traps' + 'snmp-server host <ip>'. Compare with Inform (reliable).
hack.practice: Configure trap destination on a router. Shut an interface and watch 'show snmp' on the router — the 'SNMP trap output' counter increments. On the NMS you should see the trap arrive. Now add a firewall rule blocking UDP 162; shut another interface. The trap is silently dropped — the router doesn't care, and you lose the alert. This demonstrates the unreliability.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam trap-vs-inform is guaranteed: 'traps are acknowledged?' = NO. 'Which port?' = UDP 162. 'Direction?' = agent → manager.

4.4.b.info:
An Inform is a reliable Trap. Same direction (agent → manager), same port (UDP 162), same trigger events — but the manager must acknowledge receipt, and if no acknowledgment arrives within a timeout, the agent retransmits.

Message flow: agent builds an Inform PDU and sends it to the manager on UDP 162. Manager receives it, processes it, and sends a Response PDU back acknowledging. If the agent does not see the response within the timeout (typically 30 seconds), it retransmits, usually up to 3 times, then gives up. Retransmissions consume agent memory because each outstanding Inform must be stored until acknowledged.

When to use Informs vs Traps: use Informs for critical, can't-miss events — hardware failure, security breach, compliance-mandated alerts. Use Traps for high-volume operational events where occasional loss is acceptable — routine link flaps, low-severity thresholds. A common pattern is 'critical events via Inform to the primary NMS, operational events via Trap to a separate log-heavy destination.'

Availability: Informs were introduced in SNMPv2c. They are also available in v3. Not available in v1.

Configuration: snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 informs version 2c PUBLIC — the informs keyword tells the agent to send Informs instead of Traps to that destination. You can also configure retry count and timeout: snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 informs retries 3 timeout 15 version 2c PUBLIC.

steps: Retry if no ACK (up to N times)

hack.memory: Inform = Trap with a receipt. Agent → Manager on UDP 162, BUT the manager ACKs and the agent retries if no ACK. Reliable delivery. v2c+ only (not v1). Costs more bandwidth and agent memory. Config: 'snmp-server host <ip> informs version 2c STRING'.
hack.practice: Configure both a trap host and an inform host on the same router. Shut an interface and capture packets with Wireshark on the NMS — you'll see a single UDP 162 packet for the Trap, and for the Inform you'll see a UDP 162 from the agent followed by a UDP response back from the manager.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam staple: 'which agent-initiated notification is reliable?' = Inform. 'When was Inform introduced?' = v2c. 'Is it available in v1?' = NO.

4.4.b.info:
The Trap vs Inform comparison is the single most-tested SNMP message question on the CCNA. Both are agent-initiated notifications, both use UDP 162, both fire on the same kinds of events — but they differ on reliability.

Trap — unacknowledged, fire-and-forget, lower overhead, available in all versions (v1/v2c/v3). The agent sends one packet and is done. If the packet is lost, the event is lost.

Inform — acknowledged, reliable, higher overhead (retries + agent memory to hold outstanding notifications), available in v2c and v3 only.

When you'd pick each:

  • Use Traps when event volume is high and occasional loss is acceptable (interface up/down on hundreds of ports per hour, routine threshold breaches).
  • Use Informs when the event is critical and must not be lost (security breach, hardware failure, compliance alerts).
  • Use both in practice — critical events to the primary NMS via Inform, operational firehose to a log collector via Trap.

Exam wording traps: 'fire and forget' = Trap. 'reliable' = Inform. 'uses TCP' = neither (both are UDP). 'port 161' = neither (both use 162). 'available in all versions' = Trap only.

hack.memory: Trap = yell and run. Inform = yell and wait for nod. Both agent → manager, both UDP 162, both v2c+ (Inform ONLY v2c+). Difference = ACK. Mnemonic: 'infoRm = Reliable. tRap = Run away.'
hack.practice: Flashcard three-column: Trap / Inform / both. ACK? (no / yes / —). Reliable? (no / yes / —). Port? (162 / 162 / same). Version? (all / v2c+ / —). Drill until you can fill the card in 15 seconds.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. This is THE SNMP question. You will see a Trap-vs-Inform question on nearly every CCNA exam. Drill it.

4.4.c.info:
Community strings are the authentication mechanism for SNMPv1 and SNMPv2c. They are nothing more than shared passwords: the manager includes the string in every Get/Set packet, and the agent checks whether the string matches one configured locally.

Two access levels:

  • Read-Only (RO): permits Get, GetNext, and GetBulk. Denies Set. Used for monitoring systems that only need to read counters and status.
  • Read-Write (RW): permits everything RO does plus Set. Effectively admin access to anything exposed in the MIB.

Community strings are case-sensitive. 'Public' and 'public' are different strings. This matters because a typo in the NMS config will cause polls to fail silently — the agent logs an authentication failure and drops the packet, no response back.

Plaintext transmission — the fatal flaw. The community string travels in plaintext inside every SNMP packet. Anyone with a packet capture tool on the path (Wireshark, tcpdump, a compromised switch with port-mirroring) can extract the string in seconds. There is no encryption, no hashing, no replay protection. This is why v2c should never be used across untrusted networks.

Multiple communities per device: you can configure several communities with different access levels and different ACLs. Example:

snmp-server community NOC_READ ro 10
snmp-server community AUTOMATION_RW rw 20
access-list 10 permit 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255
access-list 20 permit host 10.10.10.5

That lets the NOC poll from its subnet using one string, while automation tooling writes config from a single bastion host with a different string.

hack.memory: Community string = password in plaintext. Two flavors: RO (read) and RW (read + write). Case-sensitive. Anyone sniffing can see it. Always pair with an ACL restricting who can use it. Never deploy without an ACL.
hack.practice: On a lab router: 'access-list 10 permit 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255' + 'snmp-server community MONITOR ro 10'. Try to poll from inside the subnet (succeeds) and from outside (fails silently). This cements the ACL pairing habit.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'how does SNMPv2c authenticate?' = community strings. 'In plaintext or encrypted?' = plaintext. 'Case-sensitive?' = yes.

4.4.c.info:
'public' and 'private' are the historical default community strings used by virtually every SNMP implementation in the 1990s. public is read-only and private is read-write. Both are printed in textbooks, scripted into automation templates, and tried first by every attacker who scans a network.

These defaults are universally known and universally exploited. An attacker who lands on a management VLAN, or even a regular user VLAN without proper ACLs, will run an SNMP scanner against the default gateway and try 'public' and 'private' as the first two guesses. If either works, they have free access to read (or write) your device.

Never use these strings in production. Pick something random, long, and mixed case. Store it in a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Doppler) and rotate it periodically. Treat it like a password, not a label.

CCNA test angle: you may be asked 'what is the default read-only community string on most SNMP implementations?' The answer is public. This is one of those questions where the 'correct' answer is itself a security anti-pattern — Cisco expects you to know the default exists, not that you should use it.

How to audit: show snmp community lists configured communities. If you see 'public' or 'private' with no ACL, remove them immediately. show snmp also shows the number of 'Bad community name' errors — a rising counter indicates someone is trying random or default strings.

hack.memory: public = default RO (everyone knows it). private = default RW (everyone knows it + admin access). NEVER use either in production. First thing an attacker tries. Exam expects you to know the defaults exist, not to use them.
hack.practice: Scan any lab device with 'snmpget -v2c -c public <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0'. If it returns the hostname, the device is exposed with defaults. Now change to 'snmp-server community MY_RANDOM_STRING_8374 ro 10' and re-scan — denied. This is a 60-second security win everyone should practice.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'default RO community?' = public. 'Default RW community?' = private. Also: 'what's wrong with using them?' = they're widely known and provide attackers with free access.

4.4.c.info:
SNMPv1 (RFC 1157, 1990) was the original version. It defined the manager-agent model, the MIB, community-string authentication, and five PDU types (GetRequest, GetNextRequest, SetRequest, GetResponse, Trap). It used 32-bit counters — fine in 1990, but a 32-bit counter on a gigabit interface wraps around in ~34 seconds, making high-speed interface monitoring unreliable.

SNMPv2c (RFC 1901-1908, 1996) kept v1's community-string authentication model — hence 'v2c' for 'community-based v2' — but added several operational upgrades:

  • GetBulk — single-packet bulk retrieval, dramatically faster than chained GetNexts.
  • Inform — acknowledged notification, reliable delivery for critical alerts.
  • 64-bit counters (HighCapacity counters — ifHCInOctets, ifHCOutOctets) for high-speed interfaces where 32 bits overflows too quickly.
  • Improved error codes — v1 had 5 error statuses; v2c added more granular codes like noSuchObject, noSuchInstance, and endOfMibView.

What v2c did NOT improve: security. The community-string, plaintext model stayed identical. That's the whole point of v3 — fixing the security hole v2c left open.

Deployment reality: v1 is effectively dead — most modern devices still support it for compatibility but nobody runs it. v2c is the most widely deployed version because it's simple, works with every monitoring tool, and the operational improvements over v1 are meaningful. v3 is mandated in regulated environments (PCI, HIPAA, government) and anywhere SNMP traffic crosses untrusted networks.

hack.memory: v1 = original, 32-bit counters, no GetBulk/Inform. v2c = same security (plaintext community) + GetBulk + Inform + 64-bit counters. v2c fixed operational problems, NOT security. 'c' in v2c = community.
hack.practice: Take two facts to the exam: (1) GetBulk and Inform are v2c+. (2) 64-bit counters are v2c+. If a question asks 'which version should you use to monitor a 10 Gbps interface without counter wrap?' the answer is v2c or v3 (for the 64-bit HC counters).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'what did v2c add over v1?' = GetBulk, Inform, 64-bit counters. 'What did v2c fix about security?' = nothing — community strings still plaintext.

4.4.c.info:
snmp-server community is the core Cisco IOS command that enables a v1/v2c agent and defines a community string with its access level. Running this single command turns on the SNMP agent on the device and opens UDP 161 for polls.

Syntax:

snmp-server community [string] [ro | rw] [access-list]

Examples:

  • snmp-server community MONITOR ro — read-only, no ACL (insecure).
  • snmp-server community MONITOR ro 10 — read-only, gated by standard ACL 10.
  • snmp-server community AUTOMATION rw 20 — read-write, gated by ACL 20.
  • snmp-server community NOC ro SNMP_ACL — read-only, gated by named ACL.

Best practice — always pair with an ACL. Without an ACL, the community string is accepted from any source IP. With an ACL, only packets from permitted sources are allowed to authenticate. Combined with long random strings, this gives you defense in depth.

Related commands:

  • snmp-server contact [name] — sets sysContact (who administers this device).
  • snmp-server location [text] — sets sysLocation (where the device is).
  • snmp-server host [ip] [version] [community] — defines a trap/inform destination.
  • snmp-server enable traps — turns on trap generation.
  • no snmp-server community [string] — removes a community.

Verification: show snmp community displays all defined communities and their ACL bindings. show running-config | include snmp shows the full SNMP config block.

hack.memory: 'snmp-server community STRING ro|rw [acl]' = the one command that turns on the v2c agent. ALWAYS add an ACL at the end. No ACL = global exposure. Verify with 'show snmp community'.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: add a router, configure 'access-list 10 permit host 10.1.1.100' + 'snmp-server community MONITOR ro 10'. From that host, poll successfully. From any other host, the poll is dropped. Remove ACL, try again from any host — now it works. This shows the ACL's job clearly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam may show this command in a scenario and ask about access level or behavior. Memorize the syntax.

4.4.d.info:
SNMPv3 (RFC 3411-3418, 2002) replaced the community-string model with the User-based Security Model (USM). Instead of a single shared password, each user has a username plus optional authentication and encryption credentials, and users are assigned to groups that define what they can see and do.

Three-layer configuration model:

  • View — a subset of the MIB tree a user is allowed to see. Defined with snmp-server view [name] [oid] included|excluded. Default view covers everything; restricted views hide sensitive branches.
  • Group — a security context: version (v3), security level (noauth/auth/priv), and which views the group can read/write/notify. Defined with snmp-server group [name] v3 [level] read [view] write [view].
  • User — the actual identity with credentials. Defined with snmp-server user [name] [group] v3 auth [md5|sha] [pass] priv [des|3des|aes] [pass].

Security features:

  • Authentication — HMAC-MD5 or HMAC-SHA proves the packet came from a user who knows the auth password and was not tampered with in transit.
  • Encryption (privacy) — DES, 3DES, or AES encrypts the payload so eavesdroppers can't read the OID values or varbinds.
  • Replay protection — every packet carries a timestamp and engine-boot counter; the receiver rejects anything too old or out of sequence.

Why this matters: v3 is the only version that meets modern security expectations. Compliance frameworks (PCI, HIPAA, NIST) effectively require v3 authPriv for any SNMP deployment. Even in pure internal networks, v3 should be preferred because 'internal' often doesn't mean 'trusted.'

hack.memory: v3 = user-based security. Three building blocks: View (what you see), Group (how you authenticate), User (who you are). Security levels stack: username-only → + auth → + encryption. Only v3 fixes the plaintext-community problem.
hack.practice:
Configure a minimum v3 authPriv setup on a Cisco router:
snmp-server view FULL iso included
snmp-server group GRP1 v3 priv read FULL write FULL
snmp-server user USR1 GRP1 v3 auth sha AUTH_PASS_LONG priv aes 128 PRIV_PASS_LONG
Verify with 'show snmp user' and 'show snmp group'. Then poll from an NMS using v3 credentials.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which SNMP version provides both authentication and encryption?' = v3 (specifically at authPriv level). 'Which model does v3 use?' = user-based security model (USM).

4.4.d.info:
noAuthNoPriv is SNMPv3's weakest security level. The packet carries a username but no authentication hash and no encryption. Anyone can spoof the packet by guessing or learning the username, and anyone on the path can read the payload in plaintext.

What this level does provide: technically, identity labeling. The agent knows which user sent the request and can enforce per-user access (different users see different views). It's a modest improvement over the v2c model because there's no single shared password — usernames can be revoked individually.

What it does NOT provide: authentication (no proof the user really sent the packet), integrity (packets can be tampered with in flight), or confidentiality (contents are readable by anyone sniffing).

When to use noAuthNoPriv: essentially never in production. It's useful for lab testing — you can get a minimal v3 config working quickly without generating auth/priv passwords. For any real monitoring, go straight to authPriv.

Configuration:

snmp-server group GRP_NOAUTH v3 noauth
snmp-server user USR_TEST GRP_NOAUTH v3

No auth or priv keywords on the user — just assign to a noauth group. Notice there's no password field at all, highlighting that there's no real security here.

Exam angle: know that noAuthNoPriv is Level 1 (weakest) of v3 and uses only a username for identification. Never call it 'secure' — it isn't.

hack.memory: noAuthNoPriv = username + zero security. Labels identity but doesn't prove it. Payload still plaintext. Never production. Mnemonic: 'noAuth noPriv = no Actual protection.'
hack.practice: Flashcard: noAuthNoPriv = level 1, USERNAME only, NO auth, NO encryption. The 'no' appears twice for a reason.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which v3 level uses only a username?' = noAuthNoPriv. 'Is it encrypted?' = NO. 'Is the sender authenticated?' = NO.

4.4.d.info:
authNoPriv is SNMPv3 Security Level 2. The packet carries a username AND an HMAC-based authentication hash, but the payload is not encrypted. This proves the sender's identity and that the packet wasn't tampered with — but anyone sniffing can still read the OID values.

Authentication algorithms:

  • HMAC-MD5-96 — the original v3 auth algorithm. Still supported everywhere but considered weak by modern standards.
  • HMAC-SHA-1-96 — stronger than MD5, supported on all modern Cisco IOS. Preferred when MD5 and SHA are both available.
  • Newer HMAC-SHA variants (SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512) — available on newer IOS and IOS XE. Use the strongest your platform supports.

What authNoPriv protects against: spoofing (attacker can't forge packets without the auth password) and tampering (any byte change breaks the HMAC and the agent drops the packet).

What it does NOT protect against: eavesdropping. A Wireshark capture still shows every OID and every value in cleartext. Counter values, interface status, configuration items exposed via SNMP — all visible.

When to use: environments where you need identity verification but don't consider the SNMP data sensitive. Rare in practice — if you care enough to add auth, you usually care enough to add encryption too. Most deployments skip this level and go to authPriv.

Configuration:

snmp-server group GRP v3 auth
snmp-server user USR GRP v3 auth sha AUTH_PASS_LONG_ENOUGH

hack.memory: authNoPriv = level 2. Identity verified (HMAC-MD5 or SHA), data still plaintext. Auth prevents spoofing; no priv means anyone sniffing reads the values. Rarely used in practice — most shops jump to authPriv.
hack.practice: Flashcard: authNoPriv = HASH present (MD5 or SHA), encryption ABSENT. Sender is verified, payload is readable. Answer to 'does authNoPriv encrypt?' = NO.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which v3 level authenticates but does NOT encrypt?' = authNoPriv. 'Auth algorithms?' = MD5, SHA.

4.4.d.info:
authPriv is SNMPv3 Security Level 3 — the strongest. The packet is both authenticated (HMAC hash proves identity and integrity) AND encrypted (payload is unreadable to eavesdroppers). This is the only SNMP security level that provides full confidentiality.

Encryption algorithms on Cisco IOS:

  • DES-56 — original v3 encryption. Broken / weak by modern standards. Do not use if anything else is available.
  • 3DES (Triple DES) — stronger than DES but slow and deprecated. Middle-ground choice on older IOS.
  • AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 — current best practice. AES-128 is the minimum; AES-256 where supported.

Authentication algorithms: same as authNoPriv — HMAC-MD5 or HMAC-SHA. SHA strongly preferred.

What authPriv protects against: spoofing, tampering, AND eavesdropping. An attacker capturing the packet sees ciphertext; without the priv password they cannot recover the OID values. Replay is also blocked by the USM timestamp mechanism.

When to use: always, in any environment where SNMP credentials or data might traverse untrusted paths — which includes most internal networks. If your compliance framework (PCI, HIPAA, NIST SP 800-53) covers the device, authPriv is effectively mandatory.

Configuration:

snmp-server view FULL iso included
snmp-server group GRP v3 priv read FULL write FULL
snmp-server user USR GRP v3 auth sha AUTH_PASS priv aes 128 PRIV_PASS
snmp-server host 10.1.1.100 version 3 priv USR

Key flags: priv in the group definition sets security level, auth sha + priv aes 128 on the user sets algorithms, and version 3 priv USR on the host tells the agent to send traps/informs to the NMS at authPriv level using that user's credentials.

hack.memory: authPriv = level 3 = the vault. Auth (SHA/MD5) + Priv (AES/DES/3DES). ONLY level that encrypts. Production standard. Use AES, not DES. Use SHA, not MD5. Config keyword: 'priv' in the group definition.
hack.practice: Build a lab authPriv user. 'snmp-server group GRP v3 priv' + 'snmp-server user USR GRP v3 auth sha AUTHPASS priv aes 128 PRIVPASS'. Poll from an NMS with those credentials. Capture a packet in Wireshark — the payload is ciphertext instead of readable OIDs. That visual cement is the point of this exercise.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which v3 level provides both auth and encryption?' = authPriv. 'Encryption algorithms?' = DES/3DES/AES (AES preferred). 'Auth algorithms?' = MD5/SHA (SHA preferred).

4.4.d.info:
SNMPv3 uses two categories of cryptographic algorithms — authentication (integrity + identity) and privacy / encryption (confidentiality). Knowing which algorithm lives in which category is a guaranteed CCNA recall question.

Authentication algorithms (used at authNoPriv and authPriv):

  • HMAC-MD5-96 — 128-bit digest, truncated to 96 bits. Original v3 auth. Supported everywhere but considered weak — MD5 has known collision attacks.
  • HMAC-SHA-1-96 — 160-bit digest, truncated to 96 bits. Stronger than MD5. Universally supported on Cisco IOS and IOS XE.
  • Newer HMAC-SHA-2 variants (SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512) — defined in RFC 7860. Supported on newer platforms. Use the strongest one your NMS can speak.

Privacy (encryption) algorithms (used only at authPriv):

  • DES-56 — 56-bit key. Trivially broken today. Legacy only.
  • 3DES (Triple DES) — effectively 112-bit security. Slow. Deprecated by NIST in 2023.
  • AES-128 / AES-192 / AES-256 — current standard. AES-128 is the minimum acceptable choice; AES-256 is best.

Key selection rule for CCNA: when given a choice, SHA over MD5, AES over DES/3DES. If the question asks 'what is the strongest configuration?' the answer involves SHA + AES-256.

Password length: Cisco IOS requires auth and priv passwords to be at least 8 characters. Longer is better — aim for 16+ character random strings stored in a secrets manager.

hack.memory: Auth = MD5, SHA. Priv = DES, 3DES, AES. Don't mix them up: MD5/SHA don't encrypt, they hash. DES/AES don't authenticate, they encrypt. Best combo: SHA + AES-256. Worst (but valid): MD5 + DES.
hack.practice: Memorize this row: 'auth = MD5 or SHA. priv = DES, 3DES, or AES.' If a question lists 'AES' in an 'authentication algorithms' list, it's wrong — AES is encryption, not auth.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which algorithms are used for SNMPv3 authentication?' = MD5, SHA. 'For encryption?' = DES, 3DES, AES.

4.4.e.info:
SNMPv1 security is effectively 'none.' Authentication is a plaintext community string; there is no encryption, no integrity protection, no replay protection. The community appears in every request and response packet and can be captured with any packet analyzer.

How v1 'authenticates': the agent compares the incoming community string to its configured list. Match = accept. Mismatch = drop (and optionally increment an error counter). That's the entire security model. If the community is right, the packet is trusted.

Specific weaknesses:

  • Plaintext: anyone on path sees the community.
  • No integrity: a man-in-the-middle can rewrite the varbinds of a Set request without detection.
  • No replay protection: a captured legitimate Set can be replayed later.
  • 32-bit counters: high-speed interfaces wrap the counter in seconds, making v1 unusable for modern monitoring.

Deployment status: Obsolete. Most modern Cisco devices still support v1 for compatibility, but you should never enable it. If you find v1 running somewhere, treat it as a finding in the next audit and migrate to v2c at minimum — ideally straight to v3.

CCNA angle: know that v1 uses plaintext community strings and 32-bit counters. Recognize that v1 is the oldest and least secure. Don't memorize its RFCs or PDU format — the exam won't go that deep.

hack.memory: v1 = original, zero security, 32-bit counters that wrap fast. Plaintext community is the 'authentication.' Obsolete. If a question mentions v1, it's usually a contrast with v2c or v3 — v1 is the baseline of 'worst case.'
hack.practice: On Linux: 'snmpget -v1 -c public <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0'. Capture the packet in Wireshark — the community string 'public' is visible in plaintext in the SNMP header. That visual alone explains why v1 is dead.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'what are v1's security weaknesses?' = plaintext community, no encryption, no integrity.

4.4.e.info:
SNMPv2c security is identical to v1 — plaintext community strings, no encryption, no integrity — with one upgrade: more-granular error codes. The 'c' in v2c stands for 'community,' specifically contrasting with other v2 variants (v2u for user-based, v2* for party-based) that were proposed and never widely implemented.

What v2c improved (and it's important):

  • GetBulk — efficient bulk retrieval (multiple varbinds per packet).
  • Inform — acknowledged notifications.
  • 64-bit counters — high-capacity counters (ifHCInOctets, ifHCOutOctets) that don't wrap on gigabit+ interfaces.
  • Better error reporting — noSuchObject, noSuchInstance, endOfMibView.

What v2c did not improve: security. The v2c designers explicitly kept the community-string model because the alternative v2 security proposals had compatibility problems. Security was deferred to v3.

Why v2c is still the most deployed SNMP version:

  • Every monitoring tool supports it.
  • The operational improvements (GetBulk, Inform, 64-bit counters) are real and meaningful.
  • Internal networks are often 'trusted enough' that teams accept the plaintext risk.
  • Configuring v3 correctly is significantly harder — views, groups, users, engineIDs.

When v2c is acceptable (debatable, but common): fully internal management VLAN, tight ACLs restricting source IPs, long random community strings rotated periodically, and no regulatory mandate for encryption. Even then, v3 authPriv is strictly better.

hack.memory: v2c = v1's security + v1's architecture + operational upgrades (GetBulk, Inform, 64-bit counters). The 'c' = community (no change to authentication model). Most widely deployed because it's simple and works. Not secure — deploy only with tight ACLs and trusted paths.
hack.practice: On any Cisco device: 'snmp-server community MONITOR ro 10'. Poll with 'snmpbulkwalk -v2c -c MONITOR <ip> 1.3.6.1.2.1.2'. Notice how fast 'bulkwalk' is compared to 'walk' — that's the GetBulk operational improvement in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'does v2c provide encryption?' = NO. 'What did v2c add?' = GetBulk, Inform, 64-bit counters. 'What did v2c NOT fix?' = security.

4.4.e.info:
SNMPv3 security is the user-based security model (USM). It introduces three independent security levels — noAuthNoPriv, authNoPriv, authPriv — and replaces the community-string shared-password model with per-user credentials.

Key building blocks:

  • User — named identity with auth password and optional priv password. Multiple users per device, each with different privileges.
  • Group — collection of users sharing the same security level and access rights. Groups point to views.
  • View — subset of the MIB tree a group can access. Lets you hide sensitive OIDs from specific users.
  • Engine ID — unique identifier per SNMP entity. Used in the key-derivation process so the same auth password produces different keys on different devices (prevents cross-device replay).

Security properties:

  • Authentication (at auth and authPriv levels) — HMAC-MD5 or HMAC-SHA. Proves identity and integrity.
  • Encryption (at authPriv only) — DES, 3DES, or AES. Provides confidentiality.
  • Replay protection — USM timestamp and boot counter reject stale packets.
  • Per-packet integrity — every packet includes an HMAC; tampering breaks the hash.

Configuration complexity: v3 is noticeably harder to configure than v2c. You need to understand views before groups, groups before users, engineID behavior during user creation, and how all three interact. This complexity is the main reason some networks still run v2c despite its security gaps.

CCNA depth: you need to know the three security levels, which algorithms each level uses, and that only authPriv provides encryption. You do NOT need to configure v3 from scratch on the exam, but you should recognize the commands if shown.

hack.memory: v3 security = USM = user-based. Three levels: noAuthNoPriv / authNoPriv / authPriv. Only authPriv encrypts. Building blocks: View → Group → User. Only v3 version with real security.
hack.practice: Memorize the stacking: level 1 = just a name, level 2 = name + HMAC, level 3 = name + HMAC + encryption. Each level adds one protection. Level 3 = production.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which SNMP version uses USM?' = v3. 'How many security levels?' = 3. 'Which provides encryption?' = only authPriv.

4.4.e.info:
This single question appears on nearly every CCNA exam: 'Which SNMP version provides encryption?' The answer is SNMPv3, specifically at the authPriv security level.

Why every other option fails:

  • SNMPv1 — community strings in plaintext, no encryption. Fails.
  • SNMPv2c — same community model as v1, no encryption. Fails.
  • SNMPv3 noAuthNoPriv — username only, no encryption. Fails.
  • SNMPv3 authNoPriv — authenticated but not encrypted. Fails.
  • SNMPv3 authPriv — authenticated AND encrypted. Correct.

Corollary questions the exam may ask:

  • 'Which version is the only one that encrypts?' → v3.
  • 'Which security level provides confidentiality?' → authPriv.
  • 'Which SNMP version uses community strings?' → v1 and v2c.
  • 'Which algorithms does authPriv use for encryption?' → DES, 3DES, or AES.
  • 'Which algorithms does authPriv use for authentication?' → MD5 or SHA.

Memory aid for the whole category: 'v3 at authPriv is the ONLY SNMP combination that encrypts.' Write this on your exam whiteboard as a one-liner during the tutorial period, and any SNMP encryption question becomes a 2-second answer.

hack.memory: Encryption = v3 authPriv ONLY. Period. Every other version / level is plaintext or auth-but-no-encryption. If the question asks about encryption / confidentiality / privacy, the answer is 'v3 at authPriv.'
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Which SNMP encrypts?' → 'v3 authPriv.' Drill 10x at the start of every study session until you can answer without thinking. This is free exam points.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. THE most common SNMP security question on the CCNA. Write it on the whiteboard first.

4.5.a.info:
Syslog defines eight severity levels, numbered 0 through 7. The scale is inverted: lower numbers mean MORE severe, higher numbers mean LESS severe. Level 0 (Emergency) is the worst; Level 7 (Debugging) is the least severe.

The full list:

  • 0 — Emergency — system is unusable, kernel panic, total failure.
  • 1 — Alert — immediate action required to prevent emergency.
  • 2 — Critical — critical condition, hardware failure, major service down.
  • 3 — Error — error condition, operation failed.
  • 4 — Warning — warning condition, something might fail.
  • 5 — Notification — normal but significant event (interface state change).
  • 6 — Informational — routine operational message.
  • 7 — Debugging — debug output, most verbose level.

Why the inverted scale exists: syslog was defined in BSD Unix where Emergency (LOG_EMERG = 0) was intentionally placed at the top of the severity hierarchy. Lower numerical values = higher importance is consistent with 'priority 0 = top priority' conventions from scheduling and OS design. Counter-intuitive today, but baked into RFC 5424 forever.

Problem / normal boundary: levels 0-4 describe problem-like events (things are broken or breaking); levels 5-7 describe normal-operation events (things happening as expected). Most monitoring systems alert on 0-4 and log-only on 5-7.

hack.memory: 0-7, LOW number = HIGH severity (inverted). 0 = dying. 7 = chatty. Boundary: 0-4 = problems, 5-7 = normal. Mnemonic: Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily.
hack.practice: Write the mnemonic and numbers from memory every morning for a week. 'Every(0) Awesome(1) Cisco(2) Engineer(3) Will(4) Need(5) Ice(6) Daily(7).' The letter of each word matches the first letter of the level name: E-A-C-E-W-N-I-D. Drill until automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. GUARANTEED exam question. Write the mnemonic on the whiteboard first thing.

4.5.a.info:
The universally recommended syslog mnemonic is 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice cream Daily.' The first letter of each word matches the first letter of the corresponding severity level name in order 0-7.

Mapping:

  • Every → Emergency (0)
  • Awesome → Alert (1)
  • Cisco → Critical (2)
  • Engineer → Error (3)
  • Will → Warning (4)
  • Need → Notification (5)
  • Ice → Informational (6)
  • Daily → Debugging (7)

Why this specific mnemonic works: it's short, memorable, contains 'Cisco' and 'Engineer' (relevant to the domain), and the sequence is distinctive enough that you can reconstruct the order even if you forget a single word. Most CCNA study groups, Reddit threads, and YouTube videos converge on this exact phrasing.

Alternatives you might see: 'Eventually Every Critical Error Will Need Important Detail,' 'Each Admin Calls Every Working Night In Depression.' Pick one, stick with it, and don't switch — mixing mnemonics is the fastest way to mix up the levels on exam day.

Whiteboard brain dump: write the mnemonic with numbers (0-7) beside each word at the start of the exam. During the exam, any syslog question can be answered in seconds by glancing at the whiteboard. This is one of the highest-ROI whiteboard items for the CCNA.

hack.memory: Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily. E-A-C-E-W-N-I-D = 0-1-2-3-4-5-6-7. The two E words (Every, Engineer) land on levels 0 and 3 — that's the one spot to double-check.
hack.practice: Write the mnemonic 5 times, then write ONLY the numbers 0-7 with severity names from memory. Then test the reverse: 'what's level 5?' (Notification). 'Informational is what number?' (6). Drill all three directions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. The #1 syslog mnemonic recommended by the CCNA community. This plus the level numbers = all syslog severity questions solved.

4.5.a.info:
The most counter-intuitive thing about syslog: lower severity numbers mean HIGHER severity. Level 0 is the most severe (system unusable). Level 7 is the least severe (debugging detail). This inversion trips up beginners and is specifically tested on the CCNA.

Why it's inverted: the syslog spec originated in BSD Unix in the 1980s, where LOG_EMERG = 0 was the top priority in the priority scheduling sense. Think of it like a racing leaderboard — position 1 is the best, not position 8. Or like vehicle lanes: HOV lane 1 carries the most-important riders, lane 8 carries overflow.

Practical consequence: when you filter syslog with logging trap [level], specifying a HIGHER number captures MORE messages (because you're including higher levels, which include more messages). logging trap 7 captures everything from level 0 through 7. logging trap 0 captures only emergencies.

Common mistake: reading 'logging trap warnings' (level 4) and assuming it captures only warnings. Wrong — it captures levels 0, 1, 2, 3, AND 4 (emergency through warning). The level you specify is a floor; everything more severe (lower numbered) is also included.

Memory anchor sentence: 'ZERO is HERO' — the most important level has the smallest number. Say it aloud once and the inversion stops feeling weird.

hack.memory: ZERO is HERO. Lowest number = most severe. Highest number (7) = most verbose debug chatter. Inverted scale. When in doubt: 'the system is unusable' is as bad as it gets, and that's 0.
hack.practice: Quiz yourself: 'which is more severe, level 2 or level 5?' (2). 'Level 3 or level 0?' (0). 'Which number is Debugging?' (7). 'Which number is worst?' (0). Repeat until the inversion feels natural.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which is MORE severe, level 3 or level 5?' → level 3. 'Which level is the most severe?' → 0 (Emergency).

4.5.a.info:
logging trap [level] sets the severity threshold for syslog messages sent to the remote syslog server. Despite the name, 'trap' here has nothing to do with SNMP traps — it's legacy syslog terminology for 'send to a remote server.'

Syntax:

logging trap [number 0-7 | name]

Examples:

  • logging trap 4 — sends levels 0-4 (Emergency through Warning).
  • logging trap warnings — same as above (name form).
  • logging trap 6 — sends levels 0-6 (adds Notification and Informational).
  • logging trap debugging — sends everything (levels 0-7).
  • logging trap 0 — sends only Emergency messages.

How the filter works: the specified level is a floor / upper bound of severity. Any message at that level or lower-numbered (more severe) is sent. Any message at a higher-numbered (less severe) level is dropped for that destination.

Production default recommendation: logging trap informational (level 6). This captures everything operationally interesting (problems + state changes + routine events) without the debug firehose. Level 7 is appropriate only during active troubleshooting because debug output can flood a syslog server quickly.

Per-destination levels: you can configure different thresholds for different destinations with the discriminator feature, or by configuring multiple logging host commands. This lets you send critical events to pager-integrated servers while sending the verbose firehose to log-archival servers.

items: Includes that level + all more severe

hack.memory: 'logging trap <level>' = syslog server filter. The level is a FLOOR — captures THAT level + all lower-numbered (more severe). 'trap 4' = 0,1,2,3,4. 'trap 6' = 0-6. 'trap 7' = everything. 'trap 0' = only emergencies.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'logging trap 4 captures which levels?' → 0-4 (Emergency through Warning). 'logging trap informational?' → 0-6. 'logging trap debugging?' → 0-7. Drill 5x then reverse: 'to capture levels 0-5, set logging trap to?' → 5 (Notification).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Heavily tested: 'if logging trap is set to 4, which messages reach the server?' → 0-4. Never just one level — always that level + all more severe.

4.5.a.info:
Syslog uses UDP port 514 by default for message transport from devices to a remote syslog server. Both source and destination port 514 are used in traditional syslog (though Cisco IOS uses an ephemeral source port).

Why UDP: syslog traffic can be voluminous and should never block the device sending it. UDP's fire-and-forget model means a router generating a log message doesn't wait for an ACK or maintain connection state — it just sends. If a packet is lost, the message is lost, but the device keeps running.

Consequence: syslog over UDP is unreliable. Network congestion, firewall drops, or a syslog server that can't keep up all lead to silently dropped messages. For critical events, combine syslog with SNMP Informs or with reliable syslog (TCP or TLS).

Reliable / secure alternatives:

  • Syslog over TCP — RFC 6587. Provides delivery guarantees but requires the receiver to keep up. No encryption. Often port 514 or 601.
  • Syslog over TLS — RFC 5425. Encrypted and reliable. Uses TCP port 6514. Required in compliance environments.
  • Syslog over RELP (Reliable Event Logging Protocol) — used by rsyslog for guaranteed delivery with acknowledgment.

Configuration: logging host 10.1.1.100 uses UDP 514 by default. Specify transport explicitly with logging host 10.1.1.100 transport tcp port 601 or logging host 10.1.1.100 transport tls port 6514 on IOS versions that support it.

Firewall rules: management and regular network syslog both need UDP 514 permitted from every device subnet to the syslog-server subnet. A missed firewall rule is the #1 reason 'logs aren't showing up on the server.'

hack.memory: Syslog = UDP 514 by default. Unreliable but low-overhead. Secure version = TCP/TLS 6514 (syslog over TLS). Don't confuse with: SNMP 161/162 (UDP), SSH 22 (TCP), NTP 123 (UDP).
hack.practice: On any Cisco device: 'logging host 10.1.1.100' → 'logging trap informational' → 'service timestamps log datetime msec'. On the syslog server (even tcpdump -ni eth0 udp port 514), watch messages flow in as you shut/no-shut an interface.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'syslog uses which protocol and port?' → UDP 514. 'Secure syslog?' → TCP 6514 (TLS).

4.5.b.info:
Level 0 — Emergency is the most severe syslog level. It means the system is unusable — a kernel panic, catastrophic hardware failure, or complete software crash from which the device cannot recover. In practice, Level 0 messages are extremely rare on Cisco equipment because the device usually just reboots or halts rather than log cleanly.

Example scenarios:

  • IOS crash with a stack trace before the device reloads.
  • Dual-power-supply failure on a device with no backup.
  • Kernel-level process death on IOS XE / IOS XR.

Why it's rare in logs: by the time a condition is truly 'system unusable,' the device often cannot write the log message. You're more likely to see a Level 2 (Critical) warning about the dying hardware, then silence, than a clean Level 0 message.

Filter behavior: logging trap emergencies (or logging trap 0) sends ONLY level 0 messages — effectively muting the syslog server for everything else. Rarely useful unless combined with a higher-threshold second destination.

Mnemonic anchor: 'Every' in 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer...' = Emergency = 0. First word, first number, worst condition.

hack.memory: Level 0 = Emergency = system dead. 'Every' = Emergency in the mnemonic. Rarely seen because the device often crashes before it can log. Worst possible severity.
hack.practice: Flashcard: '0 = Emergency = system unusable. First word of the mnemonic. Worst level.' That's the whole card.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Rarely tested individually — usually tested as part of the full 0-7 sequence or the inversion rule.

4.5.b.info:
Level 1 — Alert means immediate action is required. The system is still operational, but a critical component has a problem that will escalate to an emergency if not addressed. Alert is effectively 'one step before disaster.'

Example scenarios:

  • Temperature exceeding a critical threshold but the device hasn't thermal-tripped yet.
  • Power supply failure on a device with redundant supplies (one remaining).
  • Memory corruption detected, process restart pending.

Alert vs Emergency: Emergency means the system IS unusable right now. Alert means the system WILL become unusable soon if you don't act. Alert gives you a narrow window to intervene — pull the failing module, replace the hardware, schedule an emergency maintenance window.

Practical impact: well-configured monitoring systems page on-call engineers immediately on Level 0-1 messages. These are the 2 AM wake-up events. Most organizations aim for weeks or months between Level 0-1 events on a given device; if you're seeing them regularly, your hardware is failing or your alerting thresholds are miscalibrated.

Mnemonic anchor: 'Awesome' in 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer...' = Alert = 1. Second word, second number.

hack.memory: Level 1 = Alert = act NOW or it hits 0. 'Awesome' = Alert. Pages 2AM on-call. Critical window to prevent disaster.
hack.practice: Flashcard: '1 = Alert = act immediately to prevent Emergency. Second word of mnemonic. Pages on-call staff.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Rarely tested individually. Know the word + number + position in mnemonic.

4.5.c.info:
Level 2 — Critical indicates a critical condition — something serious has broken, but the system is still running, usually in a degraded state. The device functions but with reduced capacity or without redundancy.

Example scenarios:

  • One fan of a multi-fan chassis has failed; remaining fans are keeping temperature under control but there's no safety margin.
  • A hard drive in a RAID array has failed; the array is degraded but still operational.
  • Memory allocation failure forcing a process restart.
  • Hardware module degradation — for example %FAN-2-FAN_FAILED: Fan 3 has failed.

Action timeline: Level 2 events warrant alerts (but not necessarily 2 AM pages). You want to replace the failed component within days, during a maintenance window. If you wait too long, the remaining redundant components may also fail and you escalate to Level 1 or 0.

Mnemonic anchor: 'Cisco' in 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer...' = Critical = 2. Third word, third number.

Cisco syslog format reminder: %FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC: description. A Level 2 message has '2' as the middle number. Example: %SYS-2-MALLOCFAIL = SYS facility, severity 2 (Critical), MALLOCFAIL mnemonic (memory allocation failure).

hack.memory: Level 2 = Critical = something broke but we're limping. 'Cisco' = Critical. Third in the mnemonic. Hardware fan/disk/PSU failures typically land here.
hack.practice: Read real Cisco syslog in Packet Tracer or on a lab switch. Look for '%FACILITY-2-MNEMONIC' patterns — the middle digit is severity. Every Level 2 message deserves a ticket.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Know the format %FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC and that the middle digit identifies the severity level. Tested as a read-the-log-line question.

4.5.c.info:
A Cisco syslog message follows the format:

[sequence-number:] *timestamp: %FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC: description

Real example:

000127: *Apr 16 22:40:35.123: %SYS-5-CONFIG_I: Configured from console by admin on vty0 (10.1.1.5)

Field breakdown:

  • Sequence number (000127:) — optional, added by service sequence-numbers. Lets you detect dropped messages.
  • Timestamp (*Apr 16 22:40:35.123:) — added by service timestamps log datetime msec. The asterisk indicates time is approximate (clock not synced to NTP); no asterisk once NTP locks.
  • FACILITY (SYS) — subsystem that generated the message (SYS, LINK, LINEPROTO, OSPF, BGP, DHCPD, and many more).
  • SEVERITY (5) — the severity level 0-7. This is the number you look at for severity questions.
  • MNEMONIC (CONFIG_I) — short code identifying the specific event. Unique per facility.
  • description (Configured from console...) — human-readable message, often including context like user name, IP, interface.

Reading for severity — exam drill: given %LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface GigabitEthernet0/1, changed state to up, the severity is 5 (Notification). Just look at the middle number between the two hyphens.

Common facilities to recognize: SYS (system), LINK (physical interface), LINEPROTO (line protocol — L2 interface state), OSPF, BGP, DHCPD, SEC (security), FAN, POWER, TEMP.

hack.memory: Cisco syslog format: %FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC: description. Middle number = severity. Example: %SYS-5-CONFIG_I → severity 5 (Notification). Always focus on the digit between the two hyphens.
hack.practice: Read 10 real syslog lines from 'show logging' on any Cisco device. For each, call out the facility, severity number, mnemonic, and whether the severity is in the problem zone (0-4) or normal zone (5-7). Build speed to ~3 seconds per line.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam may show a syslog line and ask 'what severity?' — read the middle number. This format knowledge is a gift: free points if you can parse it quickly.

4.5.d.info:
Level 3 — Error indicates a non-critical error condition. Something failed, but the device is still fully functional. Level 3 messages make up the bulk of actionable daily syslog traffic — real problems that need investigation but aren't hair-on-fire emergencies.

Typical Level 3 events:

  • Interface flapping (down / up / down / up repeatedly) — frequently %LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN actually at level 5, but underlying cause often at 3.
  • OSPF neighbor adjacency failure.
  • DHCP pool exhaustion — no addresses left to assign.
  • ACL deny log messages — %SEC-6-IPACCESSLOGP (at 6) or similar at 3 depending on version.
  • Authentication failures.

Error vs Warning: an Error (3) means something ALREADY failed — the operation didn't complete. A Warning (4) means something MIGHT fail if conditions continue — a preventive alert. Error = past tense ('it broke'). Warning = future tense ('it might break').

Monitoring threshold: most network operations teams set thresholds to alert on Level 3 and below (0-3). Level 3 events don't require a 2 AM page but should appear on the daily dashboard and get a ticket within the next work day.

Mnemonic anchor: 'Engineer' in 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer...' = Error = 3.

hack.memory: Level 3 = Error = 'it happened, something failed.' 'Engineer' = Error. Past tense (broke) vs Warning (might break). Bread-and-butter troubleshooting signal.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Error = 3. Engineer = Error. Past tense. Operation failed.' Versus: 'Warning = 4. Will = Warning. Future tense. Operation might fail.' The past-vs-future distinction is the memory hook.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Error vs Warning is a common trap — remember past (3) vs future (4).

4.5.d.info:
Recognizing real Level 3 log messages makes the severity-scale concept concrete. These are the kinds of lines you'll see flood into a syslog server during active incidents.

Example snippets (facility-severity-mnemonic highlights):

  • %OSPF-3-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.2 on GigabitEthernet0/1 from FULL to DOWN — OSPF adjacency lost.
  • %BGP-3-NOTIFICATION: sent to neighbor 192.0.2.1 2/2 (peer in wrong AS) — BGP session rejected.
  • %DHCPD-3-POOL_EXHAUSTED: DHCP pool LAN is exhausted — no addresses left to give.
  • %STANDBY-3-DUPADDR: Duplicate address 10.1.1.1 detected — HSRP/VRRP conflict.
  • %FAN-3-FAN_FAILED: Fan 1 has failed (note: some fan messages are Level 2 Critical depending on platform).

Reading pattern: after seeing a few dozen, you develop intuition — %OSPF-3-*, %BGP-3-*, %STANDBY-3-* are all 'an important protocol just had a problem.' You'll learn which facilities correlate with which troubleshooting paths.

Good habit: keep a personal cheat sheet of the top 10 Level 3 mnemonics you encounter on your equipment. Over time the list becomes a fast diagnostic aid.

hack.memory: Common Level 3 mnemonics: OSPF-3-ADJCHG, BGP-3-NOTIFICATION, DHCPD-3-POOL_EXHAUSTED. Pattern: protocol facility + 3 + specific code. These are the signal lines you grep for during incidents.
hack.practice: Grep 'show logging' output for '-3-' — every hit is a Level 3 Error. Read the mnemonic, guess the cause, then look up the full documentation to confirm. Repeat until your guess matches the doc 9/10 times.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Not individually tested — but useful practical knowledge for the exam's scenario questions.

4.5.e.info:
Level 4 — Warning is the classic 'yellow light' level. Something is trending in a bad direction but hasn't failed yet. Warning messages are PROACTIVE signals — ignore them and you'll likely see a Level 3 Error soon.

Typical Level 4 events:

  • Memory or CPU approaching (but not exceeding) threshold.
  • Interface error rate climbing but still below failure threshold.
  • Certificate near expiration (30/60/90 day warnings).
  • Configuration inconsistencies detected (e.g., spanning-tree type mismatch on a port).
  • Hardware temperature rising toward critical threshold.

The 4/5 boundary: Level 4 is the LAST level in the 'problem zone.' Levels 0-4 represent problem-like events (emergency / alert / critical / error / warning). Levels 5-7 represent normal operations (notification / informational / debugging). Production monitoring often alerts on 0-4 and log-only on 5-7.

Error vs Warning (reinforcement): Error (3) = operation already failed. Warning (4) = operation may fail. Past vs future.

Mnemonic anchor: 'Will' in 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need...' = Warning = 4. The 'Will' hints at future tense — something WILL fail if you don't intervene.

hack.memory: Level 4 = Warning = yellow light. 'Will' in the mnemonic = 'will fail soon.' Future tense. Boundary between problem zone (0-4) and normal zone (5-7).
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Warning = 4. Will = Warning. Future tense (might fail). Last level in problem zone. Boundary between problems and normal operations.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Know the 4/5 boundary concept — often appears as 'which levels are considered problem events vs normal events?'

4.5.e.info:
logging trap 4 (or equivalently logging trap warnings) is one of the most common syslog filter settings in production. It sends levels 0 through 4 to the remote syslog server — capturing every problem-like event (Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning) while excluding normal operational chatter (Notification, Informational, Debugging).

Why 'logging trap 4' is a popular choice:

  • Captures everything that signals a problem.
  • Excludes Level 5 interface-up/down and Level 6 routine messages that would fill the syslog server and obscure real signals.
  • Excludes Level 7 debug output (which should never be sent to a syslog server — too voluminous, risks overwhelming the collector).

Filter math reminder: specifying a level captures that level AND all more severe (lower numbered). logging trap 4 = levels 0+1+2+3+4. Not just level 4.

When to use a different level:

  • logging trap 6 (informational) — Cisco default, includes Level 5 and 6 for full operational context. Larger volume but gives you state changes and confirmations.
  • logging trap 7 (debugging) — use only during active troubleshooting. Can flood the server.
  • logging trap 3 (errors) — only actual problems, no proactive warnings. Useful when log volume is a concern.

Exam drill: 'logging trap 4 captures which levels?' → 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 (Emergency through Warning). Never just level 4 alone.

hack.memory: 'logging trap 4' = levels 0-4 = all problem events. Common production setting. 'trap' = remote syslog server filter. The level you specify = floor, captures that + all more severe.
hack.practice: Configure on a router: 'logging host 10.1.1.100' → 'logging trap 4'. Generate events at different levels (shut interface = 5, debug something = 7). Only 0-4 arrive at the server. Change to 'logging trap 6' — now 5 and 6 also arrive.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Key exam question: 'if logging trap 4 is set, which levels go to the server?' → 0-4 (all problem levels).

4.5.f.info:
Level 5 — Notification marks normal but significant events. These are not errors or warnings — they're expected system behavior worth noting. Most Cisco state change messages are classified at this level.

Classic Level 5 events:

  • %LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface GigabitEthernet0/1, changed state to up — interface coming online.
  • %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.2 on Gi0/1 from LOADING to FULL — OSPF neighbor established.
  • %SYS-5-CONFIG_I: Configured from console by admin on vty0 — configuration saved.
  • %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 10.1.1.2 Up — BGP neighbor up.

The 4/5 boundary: Level 4 is the last 'problem' level; Level 5 is the first 'normal operation' level. Many monitoring systems use this boundary to decide whether to page, alert, or just log.

Why 5 matters operationally: these are the messages that tell you 'something changed on the network' without any value judgment. An interface came up (good), OSPF neighbor formed (good), someone saved config (good — or bad, depending on authorization). You'll read Level 5 messages constantly during troubleshooting to reconstruct event timelines.

Mnemonic anchor: 'Need' in 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need...' = Notification = 5. 'Need to know' = worth notifying about.

hack.memory: Level 5 = Notification = 'FYI, state changed.' 'Need' to know. First level in the normal-ops zone (5-7). Classic Level 5: interface up/down (LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN), OSPF neighbor up (OSPF-5-ADJCHG), config save (SYS-5-CONFIG_I).
hack.practice: Run 'show logging' on a Cisco device. Scan for '-5-' messages — these are the state-change records. Try shutting and un-shutting an interface; you'll generate multiple Level 5 UPDOWN messages. Watch the timestamps line up with your actions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Classic test: 'what severity is an OSPF neighbor up message?' → 5. Know Level 5 = state changes.

4.5.f.info:
Memorizing the most common Level 5 mnemonics helps you parse Cisco logs at a glance. These three are worth knowing by heart:

  • %LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN — Layer 2 line protocol state change on an interface. Fires every time an interface's L2 protocol goes up or down (which usually tracks L1 state but not always — think shutdown administratively down vs cable pulled).
  • %LINK-3-UPDOWN — Layer 1 physical link state change. Note this is at Level 3 (Error), not Level 5. A LINK-3 and LINEPROTO-5 pair together when an interface goes down.
  • %OSPF-5-ADJCHG — OSPF neighbor adjacency state change (Init → 2-Way → Exstart → Exchange → Loading → FULL). Fires on every state transition; the final 'FULL' message means the neighbor is fully operational.
  • %SYS-5-CONFIG_I — configuration change. Logged any time someone saves or reloads the config. Useful for audit trails.

Why this matters: during an outage, grep the syslog server for these four mnemonics around the event time. You'll get a fast timeline: 'interface flapped at 22:40:35, OSPF neighbor dropped at 22:40:38, config changed at 22:41:02.'

Note on severity of LINK vs LINEPROTO: Cisco classifies the physical LINK going down as more severe (Level 3) than the LINEPROTO going down (Level 5) — a physical failure is more alarming than a L2 protocol change. This is one of the few intentional severity-level asymmetries in Cisco syslog and sometimes appears on the exam.

hack.memory: Top Level 5 mnemonics: LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN (L2 interface), OSPF-5-ADJCHG (OSPF neighbor), SYS-5-CONFIG_I (config save). Bonus trap: LINK-3-UPDOWN (Level 3 — physical is more severe than L2 protocol).
hack.practice: Greppable cheat sheet: during any lab incident, grep 'show logging' for '-5-UPDOWN' and '-5-ADJCHG' — instant timeline of what came up or went down and when.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam may ask to identify which message is Level 5 vs Level 3 — remember LINK-3 (physical) and LINEPROTO-5 (protocol) are NOT the same level.

4.5.g.info:
Level 6 — Informational is routine operational logging. Nothing is wrong, nothing is changing, the device just records normal activity for reference. This is the default logging level on most Cisco devices for console, buffer, and server destinations.

Typical Level 6 events:

  • Successful login and logout on SSH or console.
  • Commands executed in privileged EXEC (with archive log config).
  • ACL match logging (when log keyword is on the ACE).
  • Routine interface counters and usage statistics.

Why Level 6 is the Cisco default: balancing signal and noise. Level 6 captures enough context for post-incident troubleshooting without the verbose firehose of Level 7 debugging. Most production logging configurations use logging trap informational and logging buffered informational.

The 5 vs 6 trap: students frequently swap Notification (5) and Informational (6). Memory trick — in the mnemonic, 'Need' comes before 'Ice,' so Notification is before Informational. Also: Notification tends to be about state changes; Informational tends to be about routine activity (commands, successful auth).

Mnemonic anchor: 'Ice cream' in 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice cream Daily' = Informational = 6. Seventh word (some count 'Ice cream' as one), sixth level.

hack.memory: Level 6 = Informational = routine logging. 'Ice cream' = Informational. Default Cisco level for console and buffer. Common exam trap: swapping 5 and 6. N comes before I (Need before Ice), so Notification = 5, Informational = 6.
hack.practice: Flashcard: '6 = Informational. Ice cream. Routine events. Cisco default level.' Versus '5 = Notification. Need. State changes.' Drill both together to lock in the ordering.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Tested trap: 'Informational is what number?' (6). 'Notification is what number?' (5). Don't swap them.

4.5.g.info:
The Notification (5) vs Informational (6) mix-up is the most common syslog mistake on the CCNA. Students know the mnemonic but fumble the last two 'normal' levels because they sound similar.

Memory tricks that work:

  • Alphabetical order in the mnemonic: 'Need' (5) comes before 'Ice' (6). N before I. The two words are in the mnemonic in that specific order, and the numbers go up 5→6.
