  • Semantic difference: Notification = something noteworthy happened (state changed). Informational = something routine was recorded (command logged, login succeeded). 'Noteworthy' sounds more significant than 'routine,' and Notification is indeed lower-numbered (more severe) than Informational.
  • N is more severe than I: use alphabet position as a tiebreaker — N (14th letter) comes before I (9th letter) in the syslog severity order, even though N > I alphabetically. If this confuses you, stick with the mnemonic-order trick instead.

Don't use: the 'I is informational, I is bigger' trick — this conflicts with the inverted scale and will confuse you under pressure.

Quick-fire drill: 'Notification?' (5). 'Informational?' (6). 'Level 5?' (Notification). 'Level 6?' (Informational). Repeat 10x; exam-ready in 60 seconds.

hack.memory: N before I in the mnemonic. Notification = 5 = state changed. Informational = 6 = routine record. If unsure, fall back to: 'N comes before I alphabetically in the mnemonic sequence — Need Ice-cream, not Ice Need-cream.'
hack.practice: Flashcard drill 10x: '5 = ?' → Notification. '6 = ?' → Informational. 'Notification = ?' → 5. 'Informational = ?' → 6. Automate both directions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. The exam's favorite syslog trap. Drill until you can't miss it.

4.5.h.info:
Level 7 — Debugging is the most verbose and least severe syslog level. Debug messages are generated by debug commands and show granular, real-time protocol behavior — every packet, timer, state change, and decision the OS makes. Debug is the troubleshooting firehose.

Critical warning — never leave debug enabled on production:

  • Debug commands generate enormous volumes of output.
  • They consume CPU cycles that would otherwise process real traffic.
  • Some debugs (debug all, debug ip packet without filters) can crash a production router within seconds.
  • Debug output sent to a syslog server can flood the network and the server.

Common useful debug commands:

  • debug ip icmp — shows every ICMP echo/reply (ping troubleshooting).
  • debug ip ospf events — OSPF neighbor events and state changes.
  • debug ip nat — NAT translation creation and use.
  • debug ip dhcp server events — DHCP server behavior.
  • debug standby events — HSRP state changes.

Safe usage pattern: (1) plan which debug to enable, (2) enable terminal monitor if using SSH/Telnet, (3) run the debug BRIEFLY, (4) immediately run undebug all (or u all) when done. Set no logging console temporarily if debug output is breaking your terminal.

Mnemonic anchor: 'Daily' in 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice cream Daily' = Debugging = 7. Last word, last level, most verbose.

hack.memory: Level 7 = Debugging = firehose. 'Daily' = Debug. LEAST severe (paradoxically because highest number). NEVER leave on in production — 'debug ip packet' can crash a router. Always 'undebug all' (or 'u all') when done.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: (1) 'debug ip icmp' on a router, (2) ping across from another device, (3) watch the debug output flood the console, (4) immediately 'undebug all'. Feel the volume — that's a slow day on a quiet router. Imagine it on a production device under load.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'least severe syslog level?' → 7. 'Most verbose?' → 7. 'Which should not be left on in production?' → 7/debugging. All same answer.

4.5.h.info:
debug commands enable detailed real-time logging at Level 7. They're entered in privileged EXEC mode (not config mode) and produce output immediately.

Syntax pattern: debug <protocol> <subset>. The more specific the subset, the less output is generated. Always prefer specific over broad.

Examples — increasingly dangerous:

  • debug ip icmp — safe. ICMP is low-volume; only affects when someone is pinging.
  • debug ip ospf events — safe. Only fires on OSPF state changes.
  • debug ip rip — moderate. Every RIP update (every 30s) logs.
  • debug ip packet — DANGEROUS. Logs every packet. Can crash a busy router. Always use an ACL: debug ip packet 101 where ACL 101 is narrow.
  • debug all — EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Enables ALL debugs. Will crash a production router. Never use.

Turning off:

  • no debug <specific-debug> — turn off one specific debug.
  • no debug all — turn off all debugs (recommended at end of troubleshooting).
  • undebug all or u all — shorthand for no debug all.

Debug output destinations: by default, debug messages go to the console. If you're connected via SSH/Telnet, run terminal monitor first to see debug output on your VTY. Debug can also flow to the buffer and to remote syslog servers if logging buffered debugging and logging trap debugging are set — but again, don't do this on production.

Verification: show debug or show debugging lists all currently enabled debug commands. Check this before walking away from a terminal — a lingering debug can silently kill a device after you log off.

hack.memory: debug commands = Level 7 output. Entered in privileged EXEC. Always pair with 'undebug all' when done. Check 'show debug' before logging off. 'debug all' = router-killer. 'debug ip packet' without ACL = router-killer. Be specific.
hack.practice: Safe practice pattern: (1) 'debug ip icmp' → (2) generate a single ping → (3) 'undebug all' → (4) 'show debug' (should show nothing). Repeat 3x until the 'undebug all + verify' habit is automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam rarely tests specific debug commands but may ask 'which command turns off all debugging?' → 'undebug all' or 'no debug all'.

4.5.i.term: 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily'
4.5.i.info:
This is the canonical CCNA syslog mnemonic, recommended by Jeremy's IT Lab, Wendell Odom's OCG, Reddit r/ccna, Boson practice exams, and virtually every CCNA study community. Eight words, one per severity level, first letter matches the level name.

Full mapping with numbers:

  • Every = Emergency = 0
  • Awesome = Alert = 1
  • Cisco = Critical = 2
  • Engineer = Error = 3
  • Will = Warning = 4
  • Need = Notification = 5
  • Ice cream = Informational = 6
  • Daily = Debugging = 7

Why this mnemonic is the best one:

  • Short (8 words).
  • Contains 'Cisco' and 'Engineer' (domain-relevant, sticks in memory).
  • Each word maps to exactly one unique initial letter.
  • The two 'E' words (Every, Engineer) land on non-adjacent levels (0 and 3), so they're easy to keep distinct.

Practice technique: write it 5 times from memory. Then test backwards — given a level number, name the severity; given a severity name, give the number. Drill both directions until you answer instantly.

Whiteboard strategy: during the CCNA exam tutorial (before the clock starts), write this mnemonic plus the numbers 0-7. Also write your AD (administrative distance) values. These two notes alone answer 5-10+ exam questions.

hack.memory: EVERY AWESOME CISCO ENGINEER WILL NEED ICE CREAM DAILY. E-A-C-E-W-N-I-D = 0-1-2-3-4-5-6-7. Two E-words at positions 0 (Every) and 3 (Engineer). Whiteboard this on exam day.
hack.practice: Morning drill: write the mnemonic + numbers from memory. Evening drill: quiz yourself in both directions — 'level 4?' → Warning. 'Critical?' → 2. Do this every day for a week before the exam.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. THE mnemonic. Every CCNA candidate should have this automatic by exam day.

4.5.i.info:
The brain dump is the single highest-leverage exam-day tactic for the CCNA. During the short tutorial period BEFORE the official test clock starts, write critical reference material on your provided whiteboard. Then during the exam, those items are free lookups — no recall pressure.

What to brain-dump:

  1. Syslog severity mnemonic + numbers: Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice cream Daily, with 0-7 beside each word.
  2. Administrative Distance (AD) values: 0 Connected, 1 Static, 20 eBGP, 90 EIGRP, 110 OSPF, 115 IS-IS, 120 RIP, 170 EIGRP-ext, 200 iBGP, 255 Unknown.
  3. Subnet chart: /24-/30 mask-to-hosts table or common CIDR values.
  4. Port numbers: 22 SSH, 23 Telnet, 25 SMTP, 53 DNS (TCP+UDP), 67/68 DHCP, 69 TFTP, 80 HTTP, 110 POP3, 123 NTP, 143 IMAP, 161/162 SNMP, 443 HTTPS, 514 syslog, 6514 syslog-TLS.
  5. OSPF area types and their restrictions.
  6. Wildcard masks for common networks.

Why this works: the whiteboard is the only persistent memory aid you get during the exam. Writing during the tutorial is stress-free — you're not under the clock. Reading during the exam takes 5 seconds vs 30+ seconds of recall effort on a tough question.

Practice recommendation: during every full-length practice exam (Boson, Pearson), do the brain dump in the first 2-3 minutes. Build the habit so it's automatic on test day. By exam week, you should be able to write all 6 items in under 3 minutes.

Note: the whiteboard is usually erasable. If you run out of space, don't erase the brain dump — use the margins for working out subnet math and wildcard masks.

hack.memory: Brain dump = write reference on whiteboard during tutorial period BEFORE exam clock starts. Syslog mnemonic + AD values + port numbers = 5-10 free exam points. Practice this during every mock exam.
hack.practice: During your next practice exam, do the brain dump in the first 2-3 minutes. Time yourself. Goal: all 6 items in under 3 minutes by exam week.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41 + general exam strategy. Wendell Odom OCG final chapters. This technique is recommended by nearly every CCNA instructor.

4.5.j.info:
Cisco IOS can send syslog messages to four different destinations, each with its own independent severity filter:

  1. Console — real-time display on the physical console port. Command: logging console [level].
  2. Monitor (VTY) — real-time display on remote terminal sessions (SSH/Telnet). Command: logging monitor [level] + terminal monitor in each VTY session.
  3. Buffered (RAM) — stored in device RAM for later viewing. Command: logging buffered [size] [level]. View with show logging. Lost on reboot.
  4. Syslog Server (remote) — sent over the network to a central syslog server. Command: logging host [ip] + logging trap [level]. Uses UDP 514. Only destination that survives reboot.

Independence: each destination has its own severity filter. You can send Level 0-6 to the buffer for detailed local history, Level 0-4 to the remote server to reduce traffic, Level 0-7 to the console for active troubleshooting. These are configured separately.

Reboot persistence: only the remote syslog server survives a reboot. Console output is ephemeral (shown once, then gone unless you're capturing). Monitor output is per-session. Buffer is RAM-only. For any message you need to preserve, it MUST reach the remote server.

Recommended production setup:

  • logging buffered 32768 informational — keep a 32KB RAM buffer at Level 6 for local troubleshooting.
  • logging host 10.1.1.100 + logging trap informational — send Level 0-6 to the central syslog server.
  • no logging console or logging console critical — reduce console noise.
  • logging monitor debugging — allow full verbosity on VTY for debug sessions.

children.name: Syslog Server — remote, UDP 514, SURVIVES reboot

hack.memory: Four destinations: Console, Monitor (VTY), Buffer (RAM), Syslog Server. Only SERVER survives reboot. Each destination has its OWN severity filter. Mnemonic: CMBS — 'Cisco Makes Better Switches.'
hack.practice: Configure all four destinations on a router with different levels: 'logging console warnings' (0-4), 'logging monitor informational' (0-6), 'logging buffered 32768 debugging' (0-7), 'logging host <ip>' + 'logging trap notifications' (0-5). Run 'show logging' to verify each.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Heavily tested: 'which destination survives reboot?' → remote syslog server only. 'Which port?' → UDP 514.

4.5.j.info:
logging console [level] controls what appears on the physical console port — the rollover cable connection to the device. By default Cisco IOS enables console logging at Level 7 (debugging), which means every message gets displayed.

Why console logging matters: the console is the only out-of-band management interface. If SSH is broken (bad ACL, revoked credential, network outage), the console is how you recover the device. Console logs help diagnose boot-time issues and network-management failures.

The too-much-noise problem: default Level 7 console logging means any debug output (which you might enable during troubleshooting) interrupts anything you type. In the middle of typing a configuration command, a debug line can splash across your screen and make the CLI unreadable. Common fixes:

  • no logging console — turn console logging off entirely during a troubleshooting session.
  • logging console warnings — only show problem-level messages (0-4).
  • logging synchronous (under line console 0) — redraw your typed command after an interrupting log message, so you don't lose your place.

Common syntax variants:

  • logging console — turn on at default level (7).
  • logging console [level-name] — set a specific level (e.g., logging console errors).
  • logging console [0-7] — numeric form.
  • no logging console — disable entirely.

hack.memory: 'logging console <level>' = what shows on the physical console port. Default = 7 (everything). Use 'logging synchronous' on line console 0 to fix CLI interruption. 'no logging console' to mute during long config sessions.
hack.practice: On a lab router: connect via console, turn on 'debug ip icmp', ping from another device. Watch debug output splash your terminal. Now 'line console 0' + 'logging synchronous' — retype a command while pinging continues; the line redraws cleanly. That's the habit.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. 'logging synchronous' often appears on exam as a line-configuration command. Know it fixes CLI interruption from log messages.

4.5.j.info:
logging monitor [level] controls what appears on VTY sessions — SSH and Telnet connections. VTY stands for 'virtual teletype' and represents the remote CLI sessions on the device.

Two-step activation: unlike console logging which shows messages automatically, VTY logging requires TWO configuration steps:

  1. Global config: logging monitor [level] — sets what severity can flow to VTY sessions.
  2. Per-session EXEC command: terminal monitor — activates log display in the current SSH/Telnet session. This command must be issued every time you reconnect; it does not persist.

Why the two-step design: not every remote session wants to see log output. An operator running a scripted change doesn't want debug splashes in their script output. A troubleshooter does. terminal monitor is opt-in per session.

Common combinations:

  • logging monitor debugging (globally) + terminal monitor (per session) — see all output while actively troubleshooting.
  • logging monitor warnings — only show significant events even when terminal monitor is on.
  • terminal no monitor — disable log display in the current session.

Gotcha: 'I ran debug ip icmp in SSH but see nothing' — 9 times out of 10 the fix is terminal monitor. The debug IS running; you just aren't seeing its output in that VTY session.

hack.memory: 'logging monitor <level>' = what CAN show on VTY. 'terminal monitor' = what DOES show on THIS SESSION. Two-step. Per-session. Reset on disconnect. Common trap: 'my debug output disappeared' → 'terminal monitor' was never issued.
hack.practice: SSH to a lab router. Run 'debug ip icmp' without 'terminal monitor' — nothing visible. Now 'terminal monitor' — debug output appears. Disconnect and reconnect — debug is still running (show debug confirms) but you see nothing until 'terminal monitor' again.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam may ask 'what command enables syslog display on an SSH session?' → 'terminal monitor'.

4.5.j.info:
logging buffered [size] [level] stores log messages in the device's RAM. Unlike console and monitor output (which is displayed once and gone), buffered messages can be reviewed later with show logging. This is the primary local logging mechanism for Cisco devices.

Syntax:

logging buffered [size-in-bytes] [level]

Examples:

  • logging buffered — use defaults (8192 bytes, level 7).
  • logging buffered 16384 — 16 KB, default level 7.
  • logging buffered 32768 informational — 32 KB, levels 0-6.
  • logging buffered 65536 debugging — 64 KB, everything.

Circular buffer: when the buffer fills, the oldest messages are overwritten by newer ones. A larger buffer retains more history but consumes more RAM. Typical sizing: 32-64 KB on enterprise switches, 128 KB or more on routers with heavy logging.

Lost on reboot: buffered logs live only in RAM. A device reboot (intentional or crash) erases the buffer. Anything you need to preserve must reach a remote syslog server. This is the single most important distinction from the syslog server destination.

Viewing commands:

  • show logging — display the entire buffer.
  • show logging | include CONFIG — filter for config change messages.
  • show logging | begin Apr 16 — start output from a specific date.
  • clear logging — empty the buffer.

Useful tactic: before a risky change, run clear logging so the buffer contains only post-change messages. Then any errors are easy to find.

hack.memory: 'logging buffered <size> <level>' = RAM storage. View with 'show logging'. LOST on reboot. Size in bytes. Common: 32768 or 65536. Use 'clear logging' before risky changes to get a clean slate.
hack.practice: On a lab router: 'logging buffered 32768 informational'. Shut an interface, un-shut it. Run 'show logging' — see the message history. Reboot the router, log back in, 'show logging' — history is gone. This cements 'RAM = ephemeral.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which command shows the local syslog history?' → 'show logging'. 'Where is it stored?' → RAM buffer. 'Does it survive reboot?' → NO.

4.5.j.info:
logging host [ip] (older form logging [ip]) tells the device to send syslog messages to a remote server. Paired with logging trap [level], which sets the severity filter for that destination.

Minimum production config:

logging host 10.1.1.100
logging trap informational
service timestamps log datetime msec

Line-by-line:

  • logging host 10.1.1.100 — send syslog to 10.1.1.100 on UDP 514.
  • logging trap informational — only levels 0-6 (skip verbose debugging).
  • service timestamps log datetime msec — add timestamps with millisecond precision to every message. ESSENTIAL — without timestamps, correlating logs across devices is almost impossible.

Survives reboot: messages sent to the remote server are stored on the server, so device reboots do not lose history. This is the only destination with persistent history.

Multiple servers for redundancy: you can configure multiple logging host commands; messages go to all of them. Common pattern: one primary server in the data center, one secondary at a DR site.

Transport options on newer IOS:

  • Default: UDP 514 (plaintext).
  • logging host 10.1.1.100 transport tcp port 601 — TCP for reliable delivery (RFC 6587).
  • logging host 10.1.1.100 transport tls port 6514 — TLS for encrypted transport (RFC 5425).

Open-source syslog server choices: rsyslog (default on most Linux), syslog-ng (flexible config, good filtering), Graylog (web UI, analytics), Splunk (commercial, enterprise-scale), ELK/Loki stack (modern log aggregation platforms).

hack.memory: 'logging host <ip>' + 'logging trap <level>' + 'service timestamps log datetime msec' = the essential remote-syslog trio. UDP 514 default. Only destination that survives reboot. Add second host for redundancy.
hack.practice: Install rsyslog on a Linux VM (default on Ubuntu). Configure Cisco router with 'logging host <vm-ip>', 'logging trap informational', 'service timestamps log datetime msec'. Shut an interface and tail '/var/log/remote-hosts' on the VM to see the message arrive with a timestamp.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam gold: 'which destination survives reboot?' → syslog server. 'Port?' → UDP 514. 'Secure version?' → TCP 6514 (TLS).

4.5.k.info:
logging host [ip] is the Cisco command that defines a remote syslog server destination. On older IOS the form is simply logging [ip]; the two are equivalent and both still work on modern IOS.

Syntax variations:

  • logging host 10.1.1.100 — send to that IP on default UDP 514.
  • logging 10.1.1.100 — legacy short form, same behavior.
  • logging host 10.1.1.100 transport udp port 514 — explicit (default).
  • logging host 10.1.1.100 transport tcp port 601 — TCP for reliability.
  • logging host 10.1.1.100 transport tls port 6514 — TLS for encryption.

Multiple servers: configuring multiple logging host commands sends messages to every listed server. Useful for:

  • Primary + DR (disaster recovery) redundancy.
  • Splitting log destinations — critical events to an alerting server, full firehose to an archive server.
  • Temporary diagnostic capture alongside the permanent server.

IPv6 support: logging host ipv6 2001:db8::1 for IPv6 syslog servers.

Source interface: use logging source-interface Loopback0 so the source IP in syslog messages is the loopback, not whatever outbound interface. This makes server-side filtering and firewall rules predictable.

Verification: show logging shows all configured hosts and their connection state. show running-config | include logging shows the config. show ip route [syslog-server-ip] confirms reachability.

items: 'logging source-interface Lo0' for stable source IP

hack.memory: 'logging host <ip>' = add syslog server. Multiple commands = multiple servers in parallel. Pair with 'logging source-interface Loopback0' for predictable source IP. Verify with 'show logging'.
hack.practice: Configure two syslog hosts on one router: 'logging host 10.1.1.100' + 'logging host 10.1.1.101'. Generate an event (shut interface). Both servers should receive identical messages. Confirm with tcpdump or the server UI.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam knows both 'logging host <ip>' and older 'logging <ip>' — recognize both syntaxes.

4.5.k.info:
logging trap [level] sets the severity filter for all configured remote syslog servers. 'Trap' here is legacy terminology — it has NOTHING to do with SNMP traps. Syslog called remote destinations 'trap' destinations since its BSD-era origins.

Default behavior: if no logging trap is configured, the default is Level 6 (informational) — levels 0-6 go to the server.

Filter math: the level you specify is the FLOOR (upper bound of severity number) inclusive. All numerically lower levels (more severe) are also sent. logging trap 4 = levels 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.

Common production choices:

  • logging trap informational (6) — standard. Full operational visibility without debug noise.
  • logging trap notifications (5) — quieter. Excludes Level 6 chatter.
  • logging trap warnings (4) — problem-only. Reduces server load.
  • logging trap debugging (7) — everything. Use only temporarily for deep troubleshooting.

Exam-pattern questions:

  • 'logging trap 4 captures which levels?' → 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
  • 'logging trap warnings is equivalent to what numeric level?' → 4.
  • 'Which level includes debug output?' → only 7.

Caveat on per-host filters: logging trap applies to all configured hosts equally. For per-host filtering, use the discriminator feature on newer IOS to attach different filters to different destinations.

hack.memory: 'logging trap <level>' = what severity goes to the REMOTE server. Not SNMP traps — legacy syslog word. Level = FLOOR (that level + all more severe). 'trap 4' = 0-4. 'trap 6' = 0-6. 'trap 7' = everything.
hack.practice: Drill: 'logging trap informational captures what?' → 0-6. 'logging trap errors captures what?' → 0-3. 'logging trap debugging?' → 0-7. Reverse: 'to capture only Emergency/Alert/Critical, set trap to?' → 2 (critical).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Most-tested syslog command. Know that 'logging trap' = remote server filter and the level is inclusive.

4.5.k.info:
service timestamps log datetime msec is the single most important syslog best-practice command. It adds a human-readable timestamp with millisecond precision to every syslog message.

Without timestamps: messages have no time information, and correlating events across devices is impossible. You know 'OSPF flapped' and 'interface went down' but cannot tell the order of events or the gap between them. This makes post-incident analysis essentially guesswork.

With timestamps:

*Apr 16 22:40:35.123: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface Gi0/1, changed state to down
*Apr 16 22:40:37.456: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Nbr 10.1.1.2 from FULL to DOWN

You can see interface went down at 22:40:35.123; OSPF registered the neighbor loss 2.333 seconds later. Now you can reconstruct timelines.

Command variations:

  • service timestamps log uptime — default form. Shows time since device boot (less useful).
  • service timestamps log datetime — shows actual date/time.
  • service timestamps log datetime msec — adds millisecond precision (recommended).
  • service timestamps log datetime msec localtime show-timezone — adds timezone info.

Asterisk meaning: the * before the timestamp (*Apr 16 22:40:35) indicates time has NOT been synchronized via NTP. Once NTP locks, the asterisk disappears. Pair service timestamps with NTP configuration — without a synced clock, timestamps across devices won't line up and correlation breaks down.

Companion command: service timestamps debug datetime msec — same but for debug messages.

hack.memory: 'service timestamps log datetime msec' = adds date + ms precision to every log. ESSENTIAL for correlation. Pair with NTP — without NTP the times across devices don't line up. Asterisk (*) before time = NTP not synced.
hack.practice: On a lab router: observe 'show logging' BEFORE and AFTER enabling 'service timestamps log datetime msec'. The difference is dramatic. Then configure NTP and watch the asterisk disappear from timestamps.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9 + NTP. Exam gold: 'which command adds timestamps to log messages?' → 'service timestamps log datetime'. 'Why is NTP important for syslog?' → so timestamps line up across devices.

4.5.k.info:
service sequence-numbers prepends a monotonically increasing sequence number to every syslog message. With this enabled, each log line starts with a number like 000127: that counts up for every message the device emits.

Example before / after:

Without: *Apr 16 22:40:35.123: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: ...
With:    000127: *Apr 16 22:40:35.123: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: ...

Why use sequence numbers:

  • Detect dropped messages. If you see 000126, 000128, 000129, you know 000127 was dropped in transit — UDP 514 failed silently.
  • Verify order under identical timestamps. Two events in the same millisecond can be disambiguated by sequence number.
  • Audit completeness. Security auditors can confirm log continuity by verifying that sequence numbers are contiguous.

Production usage: common on devices in compliance-regulated environments (PCI, HIPAA). Less common in typical enterprise settings because the additional bytes consume log-server storage at scale. Optional but beneficial.

Related commands:

  • service sequence-numbers — enable.
  • no service sequence-numbers — disable.
  • Sequence numbers reset to 1 on device reboot.

Exam weight: minor. You may see it listed among best-practice logging commands but it's unlikely to be tested as a standalone question. Recognize the command; don't memorize details.

items: Adds counter to each log line

hack.memory: 'service sequence-numbers' = add counter (000127:) to each log line. Detects dropped messages. Resets on reboot. Niche / compliance use. Low exam weight.
hack.practice: Enable on a lab router. Note how each log line starts with a counter. Manually block UDP 514 briefly; when you restore it, sequence numbers should show a gap proving messages were dropped.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Low-probability exam item; recognize the command but don't over-invest study time.
